content.content:
1→# LLM Proxy Intelligence Capabilities
     2→
     3→**Feature**: Brain LLM Proxy -- Context Injection, Contradiction Detection, Secret Scanning, Observer Trace Analysis
     4→**Author**: Morgan (solution-architect)
     5→**Date**: 2026-03-15
     6→**Status**: Proposed
     7→**Paradigm**: Functional (per CLAUDE.md)
     8→**Depends on**: [LLM Proxy Architecture](./llm-proxy-architecture.md), [ADR-042](../adrs/ADR-042-llm-trace-as-graph-entity.md)
     9→
    10→---
    11→
    12→## 1. Overview
    13→
    14→Four intelligence capabilities layered onto the existing LLM proxy pipeline. Each operates at a different point in the request/response lifecycle and has different latency/cost characteristics.
    15→
    16→| Capability | Pipeline Position | Latency Impact | LLM Cost | Blocking |
    17→|---|---|---|---|---|
    18→| Context Injection | Pre-forward (request mutation) | +5-15ms (cached), +50-100ms (cold) | Zero | Yes (delays forward) |
    19→| Secret Scanning | Pre-forward (request validation) | +1-2ms | Zero | Yes (may block) |
    20→| Contradiction Detection | Post-response (async) | Zero (non-blocking) | Selective (Tier 2 only) | Never |
    21→| Observer Trace Analysis | Session-end (async EVENT) | Zero (non-blocking) | OBSERVER_MODEL per session | Never |
    22→
    23→**Prerequisite: Session Lifecycle Management** -- All four capabilities depend on a reliable session lifecycle. The proxy manages session start/end via inactivity timeout (primary, ADR-049), explicit end signal (optimization), and stop_reason heuristic (supplementary). Session lifecycle is detailed in [feature design](../feature/llm-proxy/design/architecture-design.md#4-session-lifecycle-management) and [ADR-049](../adrs/ADR-049-proxy-session-lifecycle-inactivity-timeout.md). It must be implemented before Capabilities 2-4 which require session identity for trace attribution, cost grouping, and Observer analysis triggers.
    24→
    25→Capability 4 (Observer Trace Analysis) is detailed in [feature design](../feature/llm-proxy/design/architecture-design.md#9-capability-4-observer-session-end-trace-analysis) and [ADR-048](../adrs/ADR-048-observer-session-end-trace-analysis.md).
    26→
    27→---
    28→
    29→## 2. C4 Component Diagram (Level 3) -- Intelligence Layer
    30→
    31→```mermaid
    32→C4Component
    33→    title Component Diagram: Proxy Intelligence Layer
    34→
    35→    Container_Boundary(proxy, "LLM Proxy Module") {
    36→        Component(anthropic_route, "Anthropic Route Handler", "TypeScript", "Orchestrates full pipeline including intelligence")
    37→        Component(identity, "Identity Resolver", "TypeScript", "Resolves workspace/session/task from request")
    38→        Component(context_injector, "Context Injector", "TypeScript", "Enriches request with graph knowledge before forwarding")
    39→        Component(secret_scanner, "Secret Scanner", "TypeScript", "Regex scan of outbound payloads for credential patterns")
    40→        Component(forwarder, "Request Forwarder", "TypeScript", "Raw byte SSE relay")
    41→        Component(contradiction_detector, "Contradiction Detector", "TypeScript", "Async post-response analysis for decision conflicts")
    42→        Component(context_cache, "Context Cache", "TypeScript", "Per-session TTL cache for workspace/project knowledge")
    43→        Component(trace_writer, "Trace Writer", "TypeScript", "Async graph writes")
    44→    }
    45→
    46→    ContainerDb(surreal, "SurrealDB", "Knowledge graph")
    47→    System_Ext(anthropic, "Anthropic API")
    48→    Container(obs_system, "Observation System", "Existing observation module")
    49→    Container(embedding_svc, "Embedding Service", "Existing embedding pipeline")
    50→
    51→    Rel(anthropic_route, identity, "Resolves request identity")
    52→    Rel(anthropic_route, context_injector, "Enriches request body")
    53→    Rel(anthropic_route, secret_scanner, "Validates outbound payload")
    54→    Rel(anthropic_route, forwarder, "Forwards enriched request")
    55→    Rel(context_injector, context_cache, "Reads cached context")
    56→    Rel(context_cache, surreal, "Queries decisions, learnings, observations")
    57→    Rel(context_injector, embedding_svc, "Embeds last user message for KNN lookup")
    58→    Rel(forwarder, anthropic, "Relays raw bytes")
    59→    Rel(forwarder, contradiction_detector, "Triggers async post-response analysis")
    60→    Rel(contradiction_detector, embedding_svc, "Embeds assistant response text")
    61→    Rel(contradiction_detector, surreal, "KNN search against confirmed decisions")
    62→    Rel(contradiction_detector, obs_system, "Creates contradiction observations")
    63→    Rel(secret_scanner, obs_system, "Creates credential-leak observations")
    64→    Rel(trace_writer, surreal, "Writes llm_call trace with intelligence metadata")
    65→```
    66→
    67→---
    68→
    69→## 3. Extended Request Pipeline
    70→
    71→```
    72→Agent Request
    73→  |
    74→  +-> [1] Parse request body (model, stream, metadata)
    75→  +-> [2] Identity Resolution (workspace, session, task)
    76→  +-> [3] Policy Evaluation (model access, budget, rate limit)
    77→  |     |-- DENY -> 403/429
    78→  |     |-- ALLOW -> continue
    79→  +-> [4] SECRET SCAN (regex against serialized body)         <-- NEW
    80→  |     |-- MATCH + policy=block  -> 403 + observation
    81→  |     |-- MATCH + policy=redact -> redact + forward + observation
    82→  |     |-- MATCH + policy=log    -> forward + observation
    83→  |     |-- NO MATCH -> continue
    84→  +-> [5] CONTEXT INJECTION (append Brain context system block) <-- NEW
    85→  |     |-- Tiered routing: fast path skips, secure path injects
    86→  |     |-- Cache hit -> use cached context
    87→  |     |-- Cache miss -> KNN query + format + cache
    88→  +-> [6] Forward to upstream (raw byte relay of enriched request)
    89→  +-> [7] Return Response to agent (SSE stream or JSON)
    90→
    91→Post-Response (async, non-blocking):
    92→  +-> [8] Extract usage (SSE buffer / JSON body)
    93→  +-> [9] Compute cost
    94→  +-> [10] CONTRADICTION DETECTION                              <-- NEW
    95→  |     |-- Tier 1: embed response, KNN against decisions
    96→  |     |-- Tier 2 (if flagged): Haiku verification prompt
    97→  |     |-- Confirmed: create observation
    98→  +-> [11] Write trace (with intelligence metadata fields)
    99→```
   100→
   101→---
   102→
   103→## 4. Capability 1: Context Injection
   104→
   105→### 4.1 Data Flow
   106→
   107→```
   108→Identity (workspace, session, task)
   109→  |
   110→  +-> Check tiered routing config
   111→  |     |-- fast_path -> skip injection, forward as-is
   112→  |     |-- secure_path -> continue
   113→  |
   114→  +-> Check context cache (keyed by session_id)
   115→  |     |-- HIT + not expired -> use cached context packet
   116→  |     |-- MISS or expired -> build fresh context
   117→  |
   118→  +-> Build context packet:
   119→  |     |-- Extract search query from last user message
   120→  |     |-- Embed query via existing embedding pipeline
   121→  |     |-- KNN search: decisions, learnings, observations (two-step pattern)
   122→  |     |-- Score + rank by priority tier
   123→  |     |-- Truncate to token budget
   124→  |     |-- Cache result (TTL from config)
   125→  |
   126→  +-> Inject into request body:
   127→  |     |-- Parse system field (string or array)
   128→  |     |-- Normalize to array of content blocks
   129→  |     |-- Append new block at end (preserves prompt cache)
   130→  |     |-- Re-serialize body
   131→  |
   132→  +-> Forward enriched request
   133→```
   134→
   135→### 4.2 Query Strategy
   136→
   137→Three signals extracted from the request, used in priority order:
   138→
   139→| Signal | Source | Purpose | Stability |
   140→|---|---|---|---|
   141→| Workspace + Project | Identity resolver (session headers) | Scope all queries | Stable per session |
   142→| Last user message | Last `user` role entry in `messages` array | Semantic search query for KNN | Changes per turn |
   143→| Tool calls | `tool_use` / `tool_result` blocks in messages | File paths for task-level context | Changes per turn |
   144→
   145→The workspace/project context (confirmed decisions, active constraints, learnings) is stable per session and cached. The semantic relevance ranking (which of those items to include) changes per turn based on the user message embedding.
   146→
   147→**Two-phase query** (leverages caching):
   148→1. **Session-scoped fetch (cached, TTL 5min)**: All confirmed decisions + active learnings + open observations for workspace. This is the candidate pool.
   149→2. **Per-turn ranking (computed per request)**: Embed last user message, compute cosine similarity against cached candidate embeddings, rank by priority tier within budget.
   150→
   151→This avoids a KNN database query on every request -- the KNN ranking happens in-memory against the cached candidate pool.
   152→
   153→### 4.3 SurrealDB Queries
   154→
   155→**Fetch candidate pool (session cache miss)**:
   156→
   157→```sql
   158→-- Confirmed decisions for workspace (no KNN, simple filter)
   159→SELECT id, summary, rationale, status, embedding
   160→FROM decision
   161→WHERE workspace = $ws AND status = "confirmed"
   162→ORDER BY created_at DESC
   163→LIMIT 50;
   164→
   165→-- Active learnings for workspace
   166→SELECT id, text, learning_type, priority, target_agents, embedding
   167→FROM learning
   168→WHERE workspace = $ws AND status = "active"
   169→ORDER BY priority DESC, created_at DESC
   170→LIMIT 30;
   171→
   172→-- Open observations for workspace (conflict + warning only)
   173→SELECT id, text, severity, observation_type, embedding
   174→FROM observation
   175→WHERE workspace = $ws
   176→  AND status IN ["open", "acknowledged"]
   177→  AND severity IN ["conflict", "warning"]
   178→ORDER BY created_at DESC
   179→LIMIT 20;
   180→```
   181→
   182→No KNN + WHERE bug encountered here -- these are simple indexed queries fetching the candidate pool. The cosine similarity ranking is computed in-memory in the proxy process against the fetched embeddings.
   183→
   184→**In-memory ranking** (per-turn, no DB call):
   185→```
   186→For each candidate in pool:
   187→  similarity = cosine(candidate.embedding, userMessageEmbedding)
   188→  score = similarity * priorityWeight[candidate.tier]
   189→Sort by score DESC
   190→Take top N within token budget
   191→```
   192→
   193→Priority weights: decisions (1.0) > constraints (0.9) > learnings (0.8) > observations (0.7).
   194→
   195→### 4.4 Injected System Block Format
   196→
   197→The Anthropic Messages API `system` field accepts either a string or an array of content blocks. Claude Code sends it as an array with `cache_control: { type: "ephemeral" }` on its blocks. The proxy MUST:
   198→
   199→1. Normalize `system` to array form (if string, wrap in `[{ type: "text", text: value }]`)
   200→2. Append a new block at the END of the array
   201→3. Never modify existing blocks (preserves prompt cache hits)
   202→
   203→**Injected block structure**:
   204→
   205→```json
   206→{
   207→  "type": "text",
   208→  "text": "<brain-context workspace=\"acme\" project=\"backend-v2\" session=\"abc-123\" injected_at=\"2026-03-15T10:30:00Z\">\n## Active Decisions\n- [d:7f3a] Standardize on tRPC for all internal APIs (confirmed 2026-03-10)\n- [d:9b2c] PostgreSQL 16 for all persistent storage (confirmed 2026-03-08)\n\n## Constraints\n- [l:4e1f] All new endpoints must include DPoP authentication (high priority)\n\n## Open Observations\n- [o:2d5a] WARNING: billing API still uses REST, contradicts tRPC decision d:7f3a\n</brain-context>",
   209→  "cache_control": { "type": "ephemeral" }
   210→}
   211→```
   212→
   213→Design choices:
   214→- **XML-like wrapper** (`<brain-context>`) with attributes for metadata -- LLMs parse XML structure reliably, and the tag name is distinctive enough to avoid collision with agent prompts
   215→- **Markdown inside** -- decisions, learnings, observations as bullet lists with short IDs for reference
   216→- **`cache_control: ephemeral`** -- marks this block for prompt caching so subsequent turns in the same session benefit from cached context (the context is stable per session)
   217→- **Short IDs** (`d:7f3a`) -- first 4 chars of the record UUID, enough for the LLM to reference back without consuming tokens on full UUIDs
   218→
   219→### 4.5 Token Budget
   220→
   221→| Item | Estimated Tokens | Max Items | Budget |
   222→|---|---|---|---|
   223→| Wrapper + metadata | ~30 | 1 | 30 |
   224→| Decisions | ~40 each | 10 | 400 |
   225→| Learnings/Constraints | ~30 each | 5 | 150 |
   226→| Observations | ~35 each | 5 | 175 |
   227→| **Total** | | | **~755** |
   228→
   229→Default budget: 1000 tokens. Configurable per workspace (200-2000 range). Budget enforcement: count items by priority tier until budget exhausted.
   230→
   231→### 4.6 Session Caching
   232→
   233→| Cache Key | Value | TTL | Invalidation |
   234→|---|---|---|---|
   235→| `ctx:{session_id}` | Candidate pool (decisions + learnings + observations with embeddings) | 5 min (configurable) | Session change, manual flush |
   236→| `emb:{hash(text)}` | User message embedding vector | 10 min | LRU eviction |
   237→
   238→Cache is in-memory (`Map`), per-process. No shared cache needed -- each proxy process handles its own sessions.
   239→
   240→---
   241→
   242→## 5. Capability 2: Contradiction Detection
   243→
   244→### 5.1 Data Flow
   245→
   246→```
   247→Stream Complete / Response Received
   248→  |
   249→  +-> Check stop_reason
   250→  |     |-- "tool_use" -> skip (intermediate loop step)
   251→  |     |-- "end_turn" -> proceed with Tier 1
   252→  |     |-- "max_tokens" -> proceed with Tier 1
   253→  |
   254→  +-> Extract analysis targets from response:
   255→  |     |-- Assistant text blocks (reasoning, decisions, explanations)
   256→  |     |-- Tool call inputs for Edit/Write/Bash (action content)
   257→  |
   258→  +-> TIER 1: Embedding similarity (every qualifying response, zero LLM cost)
   259→  |     |-- Embed extracted text (batch if multiple targets)
   260→  |     |-- KNN search against confirmed decisions (two-step pattern)
   261→  |     |-- Filter: similarity > TIER1_THRESHOLD (configurable, default 0.75)
   262→  |     |-- No matches above threshold -> done, no contradiction
   263→  |     |-- Matches found -> pass to Tier 2
   264→  |
   265→  +-> TIER 2: LLM verification (selective, Haiku-class, async)
   266→  |     |-- For each flagged decision:
   267→  |     |     |-- Prompt: "Agent action: {text}. Decision: {summary+rationale}. Contradiction? yes/no + explanation"
   268→  |     |     |-- If "no" -> debug log, discard
   269→  |     |     |-- If "yes" -> create observation
   270→  |     |
   271→  |     +-> Create observation:
   272→  |           |-- severity: "conflict"
   273→  |           |-- observation_type: "contradiction"
   274→  |           |-- source_agent: "llm_proxy"
   275→  |           |-- source_session: agent_session record
   276→  |           |-- relatedRecords: [contradicted_decision]
   277→  |           |-- text: "Agent action contradicts decision [d:ID]: {explanation}"
   278→  |           |-- embedding: from Tier 1
   279→```
   280→
   281→### 5.2 When to Check
   282→
   283→| Stop Reason | Check? | Rationale |
   284→|---|---|---|
   285→| `end_turn` | Yes | Final response -- contains reasoning and decisions |
   286→| `max_tokens` | Yes | Truncated but still contains actionable content |
   287→| `tool_use` | No | Intermediate step, will produce more output |
   288→| `stop_sequence` | No | Rare, typically control flow |
   289→
   290→Additionally, within the response content blocks:
   291→- **Text blocks**: Always analyze (contain reasoning, decisions, explanations)
   292→- **`tool_use` blocks for Edit/Write/Bash**: Extract `input` field for action content analysis
   293→- **`tool_use` blocks for Read/Glob/Grep**: Skip (read-only, no contradiction risk)
   294→
   295→### 5.3 SurrealDB Queries (Two-Step KNN Pattern)
   296→
   297→Per the SurrealDB v3.0 KNN + WHERE bug, KNN and B-tree indexed WHERE clauses cannot be combined. Use the established two-step pattern.
   298→
   299→**Tier 1 -- KNN candidate fetch + workspace filter**:
   300→
   301→```sql
   302→-- Step 1: KNN candidates (HNSW index only, no WHERE)
   303→LET $candidates = SELECT id, summary, rationale, workspace,
   304→  vector::similarity::cosine(embedding, $response_embedding) AS similarity
   305→FROM decision WHERE embedding <|20, COSINE|> $response_embedding;
   306→
   307→-- Step 2: Filter by workspace + confirmed status + threshold
   308→SELECT id, summary, rationale, similarity
   309→FROM $candidates
   310→WHERE workspace = $ws
   311→  AND status = "confirmed"
   312→  AND similarity > $tier1_threshold
   313→ORDER BY similarity DESC
   314→LIMIT 5;
   315→```
   316→
   317→Wait -- the `status` field is not selected in Step 1, and `status` filtering should happen in Step 2. Updated:
   318→
   319→```sql
   320→LET $candidates = SELECT id, summary, rationale, status, workspace,
   321→  vector::similarity::cosine(embedding, $response_embedding) AS similarity
   322→FROM decision WHERE embedding <|20, COSINE|> $response_embedding;
   323→
   324→SELECT id, summary, rationale, similarity
   325→FROM $candidates
   326→WHERE workspace = $ws
   327→  AND status = "confirmed"
   328→  AND similarity > $tier1_threshold
   329→ORDER BY similarity DESC
   330→LIMIT 5;
   331→```
   332→
   333→### 5.4 Tier 2 Verification Prompt
   334→
   335→```
   336→You are a contradiction detector. Compare the agent's action against a confirmed organizational decision.
   337→
   338→<agent_action>
   339→{extracted_text}
   340→</agent_action>
   341→
   342→<confirmed_decision id="{decision_id}">
   343→Summary: {summary}
   344→Rationale: {rationale}
   345→</confirmed_decision>
   346→
   347→Does the agent's action contradict this decision?
   348→
   349→Respond with exactly one JSON object:
   350→{
   351→  "contradicts": true | false,
   352→  "explanation": "one sentence explanation",
   353→  "confidence": 0.0 to 1.0
   354→}
   355→```
   356→
   357→- Model: Haiku-class (cheapest available, configured via `CONTRADICTION_MODEL` env var or workspace config)
   358→- Only invoked when Tier 1 similarity exceeds threshold
   359→- Confidence < 0.6 from Tier 2 -> discard (false positive gate)
   360→
   361→### 5.5 Observer Delegation
   362→
   363→The proxy performs fast, shallow detection. The Observer agent performs slow, deep analysis.
   364→
   365→| Proxy (real-time) | Observer (periodic scan) |
   366→|---|---|
   367→| Single response vs single decision | Cross-session pattern synthesis |
   368→| Embedding similarity + Haiku check | Multi-evidence reasoning chains |
   369→| Creates individual observations | Aggregates observations into suggestions |
   370→| Runs per-response (~100ms async) | Runs per-scan (~minutes) |
   371→
   372→The proxy writes `observation_type: "contradiction"` observations. The Observer picks these up on its next scan, correlates with other observations, and can escalate to suggestions or learnings.
   373→
   374→---
   375→
   376→## 6. Capability 3: Secret Scanning
   377→
   378→### 6.1 Data Flow
   379→
   380→```
   381→Parsed Request Body (serialized JSON string)
   382→  |
   383→  +-> Pattern match against credential regex set
   384→  |     |-- NO MATCH -> continue pipeline (fast path)
   385→  |     |-- MATCH -> determine action from workspace policy
   386→  |           |-- "block"  -> return 403, create observation, do not forward
   387→  |           |-- "redact" -> replace matched patterns with [REDACTED], forward, create observation
   388→  |           |-- "log"    -> forward as-is, create observation
   389→  |
   390→  +-> Create observation (on any match):
   391→        |-- severity: "conflict"
   392→        |-- observation_type: "anomaly"
   393→        |-- source_agent: "llm_proxy_secret_scanner"
   394→        |-- text: "Credential pattern detected in LLM request: {pattern_name} ({count} matches)"
   395→        |-- data: { pattern_name, match_count, action_taken }
   396→        |-- (NEVER include the actual credential value in the observation)
   397→```
   398→
   399→### 6.2 Credential Patterns
   400→
   401→| Pattern Name | Regex | Risk Level |
   402→|---|---|---|
   403→| `github_pat` | `ghp_[A-Za-z0-9_]{36}` | High |
   404→| `github_fine_grained` | `github_pat_[A-Za-z0-9_]{82}` | High |
   405→| `aws_access_key` | `AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}` | Critical |
   406→| `aws_secret_key` | `[A-Za-z0-9/+=]{40}` (preceded by `aws_secret`) | Critical |
   407→| `stripe_live` | `sk_live_[A-Za-z0-9]{24,}` | Critical |
   408→| `stripe_test` | `sk_test_[A-Za-z0-9]{24,}` | Medium |
   409→| `openai_key` | `sk-[A-Za-z0-9]{48,}` | High |
   410→| `anthropic_key` | `sk-ant-[A-Za-z0-9-]{90,}` | High |
   411→| `generic_bearer` | `Bearer [A-Za-z0-9._~+/=-]{20,}` (in message content, not headers) | Medium |
   412→| `connection_string` | `(postgres\|mysql\|mongodb)://[^\s"']+@[^\s"']+` | High |
   413→| `private_key_block` | `-----BEGIN (RSA\|EC\|OPENSSH) PRIVATE KEY-----` | Critical |
   414→
   415→**Scan scope**: The serialized request body string. This covers `messages` content, `system` prompt, and `tool_result` blocks -- anywhere a credential could leak into an LLM request.
   416→
   417→**Exclusions**: The `x-api-key` and `authorization` headers are intentionally NOT scanned -- they are expected to contain credentials (the agent's own API key being forwarded).
   418→
   419→### 6.3 Performance
   420→
   421→- Pattern set compiled once at startup (single combined regex or individual compiled patterns)
   422→- Scan runs against the already-serialized request body string (no extra serialization)
   423→- Expected latency: < 2ms for typical request bodies (5-50KB)
   424→- No false-positive LLM verification needed -- regex matches on known credential formats are deterministic
   425→
   426→---
   427→
   428→## 7. Configuration Model
   429→
   430→### 7.1 Per-Workspace Configuration
   431→
   432→Stored in a `proxy_intelligence_config` record linked to the workspace. Schema extension:
   433→
   434→```sql
   435→DEFINE TABLE proxy_intelligence_config SCHEMAFULL;
   436→DEFINE FIELD workspace ON proxy_intelligence_config TYPE record<workspace>;
   437→
   438→-- Context Injection
   439→DEFINE FIELD context_injection_enabled ON proxy_intelligence_config TYPE bool DEFAULT true;
   440→DEFINE FIELD context_injection_token_budget ON proxy_intelligence_config TYPE int DEFAULT 1000;
   441→DEFINE FIELD context_injection_cache_ttl_seconds ON proxy_intelligence_config TYPE int DEFAULT 300;
   442→DEFINE FIELD context_injection_tier ON proxy_intelligence_config TYPE string
   443→  DEFAULT "secure"
   444→  ASSERT $value IN ["fast", "secure"];
   445→
   446→-- Contradiction Detection
   447→DEFINE FIELD contradiction_detection_enabled ON proxy_intelligence_config TYPE bool DEFAULT true;
   448→DEFINE FIELD contradiction_tier1_threshold ON proxy_intelligence_config TYPE float DEFAULT 0.75;
   449→DEFINE FIELD contradiction_tier2_confidence_min ON proxy_intelligence_config TYPE float DEFAULT 0.6;
   450→
   451→-- Secret Scanning
   452→DEFINE FIELD secret_scanning_enabled ON proxy_intelligence_config TYPE bool DEFAULT true;
   453→DEFINE FIELD secret_scanning_action ON proxy_intelligence_config TYPE string
   454→  DEFAULT "block"
   455→  ASSERT $value IN ["block", "redact", "log"];
   456→
   457→-- General
   458→DEFINE FIELD created_at ON proxy_intelligence_config TYPE datetime;
   459→DEFINE FIELD updated_at ON proxy_intelligence_config TYPE datetime;
   460→
   461→DEFINE INDEX proxy_intel_config_workspace ON proxy_intelligence_config FIELDS workspace UNIQUE;
   462→```
   463→
   464→### 7.2 Tiered Routing
   465→
   466→The `context_injection_tier` field controls whether context injection runs per request:
   467→
   468→| Tier | Context Injection | Contradiction Detection | Secret Scanning | Use Case |
   469→|---|---|---|---|---|
   470→| `fast` | Skipped | Still runs (async, non-blocking) | Still runs (< 2ms) | High-throughput agents, cost-sensitive |
   471→| `secure` | Enabled | Enabled | Enabled | Production agents working on governed projects |
   472→
   473→Tiered routing is per-workspace. A workspace admin sets the tier based on their risk tolerance and latency requirements.
   474→
   475→### 7.3 Environment Variables (Global Defaults)
   476→
   477→```
   478→# Intelligence feature flags (global kill switches)
   479→LLM_PROXY_CONTEXT_INJECTION=true
   480→LLM_PROXY_CONTRADICTION_DETECTION=true
   481→LLM_PROXY_SECRET_SCANNING=true
   482→
   483→# Contradiction detection model
   484→CONTRADICTION_MODEL=<haiku-class-model-id>
   485→
   486→# Default token budget (overridden by workspace config)
   487→LLM_PROXY_CONTEXT_TOKEN_BUDGET=1000
   488→```
   489→
   490→---

   492→## 8. Trace Metadata Extension

   495→
   496→**`input` FLEXIBLE additions** (when context injection runs):

   500→  "brain_context_decisions": 3,

   512→  "contradiction_observation_ids": ["observation:7f3a..."]
   513→}

   515→
   516→This metadata enables the Observer to analyze intelligence pipeline effectiveness across sessions.
   517→
   518→---
   519→
   520→## 9. ADR-043: Context Injection Approach -- Append-Only System Block
   521→
   522→### Status
   523→Proposed
   524→
   525→### Context
   526→The proxy needs to inject Brain knowledge graph context into LLM requests. Three approaches were considered for how to deliver the context to the model. The primary constraint is prompt cache compatibility -- Claude Code uses `cache_control: { type: "ephemeral" }` on its system blocks, and modifying those blocks invalidates the cache, increasing cost and latency for every turn.
   527→
   528→### Decision
   529→Append a new system content block AFTER all existing blocks. Normalize the `system` field to array form if it arrives as a string. Mark the appended block with `cache_control: { type: "ephemeral" }`.
   530→
   531→### Alternatives Considered
   532→
   533→**Alternative 1: Modify existing system blocks (inline injection)**
   534→- Insert Brain context into the middle or beginning of the existing system prompt
   535→- **Why rejected**: Invalidates Claude Code's prompt cache on every request. The cache key includes the full system content -- any mutation forces a cache miss. At ~$3.75/MTok for cache creation vs ~$0.30/MTok for cache reads, this could increase per-session cost by 10-12x for the system prompt portion. Additionally, finding a reliable injection point inside an opaque agent prompt is fragile.
   536→
   537→**Alternative 2: HTTP header injection (X-Brain-Context)**
   538→- Pass context via custom HTTP headers, rely on the provider to include them
   539→- **Why rejected**: Anthropic's Messages API does not read custom headers as context. The model only sees `system`, `messages`, and `tools` fields. Headers are invisible to the model. Would require Anthropic to build a custom integration, which is not feasible.
   540→
   541→**Alternative 3: Prepend a user message**
   542→- Insert a synthetic `user` message at the beginning of the conversation with Brain context
   543→- **Why rejected**: Mutates the `messages` array, potentially confusing the agent's conversation state management. Some agents track message indices. Also interferes with the model's understanding of conversation flow -- a system-level context appearing as a "user" message could cause the model to treat it as a user instruction rather than background context.
   544→
   545→### Consequences
   546→- **Positive**: Zero impact on prompt cache -- existing blocks untouched, new block appended
   547→- **Positive**: Clear separation -- `<brain-context>` wrapper makes the injected block identifiable
   548→- **Positive**: Compatible with both string and array `system` field formats
   549→- **Positive**: The appended block itself benefits from ephemeral caching on subsequent turns (context is stable per session)
   550→- **Negative**: Increases total system prompt size by ~750-1000 tokens per request
   551→- **Negative**: The appended block position (last) means the model may weight it slightly less than earlier blocks -- mitigated by the distinctive XML wrapper tag
   552→
   553→---
   554→
   555→## 10. ADR-044: Contradiction Detection Approach -- Proxy Hybrid with Observer Delegation
   556→
   557→### Status
   558→Proposed
   559→
   560→### Context
   561→Agent actions can contradict confirmed decisions in the knowledge graph. Three approaches were considered for detecting these contradictions. The key trade-off is between detection speed (catching contradictions before they compound) and detection depth (reducing false positives through multi-evidence reasoning).
   562→
   563→### Decision
   564→Hybrid two-tier pipeline in the proxy (fast, shallow) with delegation to the Observer agent (slow, deep). Tier 1 (embedding similarity) runs on every qualifying response at zero LLM cost. Tier 2 (Haiku verification) runs selectively on flagged candidates. The Observer synthesizes patterns across sessions.
   565→
   566→### Alternatives Considered
   567→
   568→**Alternative 1: Observer-only detection (periodic scan)**
   569→- The Observer agent scans traces on its periodic schedule, comparing agent outputs against decisions
   570→- **Why rejected**: Detection latency of minutes to hours. An agent could implement a contradicting change across dozens of tool calls before the next Observer scan. By then, the contradiction is embedded in code and harder to remediate. The proxy sees every response in real-time and can flag contradictions within seconds.
   571→
   572→**Alternative 2: Proxy-only detection (full analysis inline)**
   573→- The proxy performs deep contradiction analysis inline, including multi-evidence reasoning
   574→- **Why rejected**: Deep analysis requires loading related decisions, prior observations, and conversation history -- too much latency and complexity for a per-response check. Would add 1-5s to every response if done inline. The proxy should be fast and shallow; deep analysis is the Observer's job.
   575→
   576→### Consequences
   577→- **Positive**: Sub-second detection for clear contradictions (Tier 1 similarity match)
   578→- **Positive**: Low false positive rate (Tier 2 Haiku verification acts as precision gate)
   579→- **Positive**: Zero latency impact on agent (entire pipeline is async, post-response)
   580→- **Positive**: Observer can aggregate proxy-created observations for deeper pattern analysis
   581→- **Negative**: Tier 2 incurs Haiku-class LLM cost per flagged candidate (~$0.001 per check)
   582→- **Negative**: Proxy detection is shallow -- cannot catch nuanced contradictions that require multi-document reasoning (Observer handles these)
   583→- **Negative**: Requires embedding computation for every qualifying response (mitigated by batching and async execution)
   584→
   585→---
   586→

   711→## 13. Quality Attribute Impact
   712→
   713→| Attribute | Impact | Mitigation |
   714→|---|---|---|
   715→| **Performance** | Context injection adds 5-15ms (cached) on hot path | Session caching, tiered routing fast path, in-memory cosine ranking |
   716→| **Performance** | Secret scanning adds ~1-2ms on hot path | Compiled regex, runs against already-serialized string |
   717→| **Performance** | Contradiction detection is fully async | Zero hot-path impact, tracked via inflight |
   718→| **Reliability** | Context injection failure must not block request | Catch-all error handler: on any failure, forward request without injection, log warning |
   719→| **Reliability** | Contradiction detection failure is non-critical | Catch-all: on failure, skip detection, log error, do not create observation |
   720→| **Security** | Secret scanner is defense-in-depth | Not a replacement for proper credential management; last resort if credentials leak into prompts |
   721→| **Cost** | Context injection is zero LLM cost | Graph queries + in-memory ranking only |
   722→| **Cost** | Contradiction Tier 2 uses Haiku-class model | ~$0.001 per check, only on flagged candidates (expected <5% of responses) |
   723→| **Maintainability** | Four independent capabilities, each optional | Feature flags at global (env) and workspace (config) level |
   724→| **Performance** | Session trace analysis runs post-session (ASYNC EVENT) | Zero hot-path impact, runs after session commits |
   725→| **Reliability** | Session trace analysis failure is non-critical | ASYNC EVENT + handler returns 200 on all errors |
   726→| **Cost** | Session trace analysis uses OBSERVER_MODEL per session | One invocation per session end, only when traces exist |
   727→| **Completeness** | Session-end gives full-session view for missing decisions and cross-trace contradictions | Complements per-request detection |
   728→
   729→---
   730→
   731→## 14. Rejected Simpler Alternatives
   732→
   733→### Alternative: MCP-only context delivery (no proxy injection)
   734→- **What**: Rely entirely on `brain init` + MCP `get_context` tool for knowledge graph context
   735→- **Expected impact**: Works for agents with Brain MCP integration already set up
   736→- **Why insufficient**: The proxy's value proposition is making ANY agent smarter without requiring MCP integration, `brain init`, or any client-side setup. MCP-only means agents without Brain integration get zero knowledge graph benefits. The proxy is the zero-config path.
   737→
   738→### Alternative: Full response buffering for contradiction detection
   739→- **What**: Buffer the entire SSE response, then analyze it before releasing to the agent
   740→- **Expected impact**: Enables blocking contradictions before the agent sees them
   741→- **Why rejected**: Destroys the streaming experience. The agent would see no output until the full response is buffered + analyzed. Adds seconds of perceived latency. Contradictions are better handled as async observations that surface in the feed/dashboard rather than blocking the agent's workflow.
   742→

1→# ADR-040: LLM Proxy as In-Process Module
     2→
     3→## Status
     4→Proposed
     5→
     6→## Context
     7→The LLM Proxy needs to intercept all agent LLM calls, write traces to SurrealDB, evaluate policies, and track costs. We must decide whether the proxy runs as a separate process/service or as a module within the existing Brain Bun server.
     8→
     9→Team size is 1-3 developers. The existing Brain server already has SurrealDB connections, inflight tracking, policy engine, and observation system. Operational maturity is early-stage (no container orchestration, no service mesh).
    10→
    11→## Decision
    12→Run the LLM Proxy as an in-process module within the existing Brain Bun server, sharing `ServerDependencies`.
    13→
    14→## Alternatives Considered
    15→
    16→### Alternative 1: Separate Bun process on a dedicated port
    17→- **What**: Standalone proxy server on port 4100, communicating with Brain via HTTP
    18→- **Expected impact**: Full isolation, independent scaling
    19→- **Why insufficient**: Doubles operational complexity (two processes to manage), requires inter-process communication for policy checks and graph writes, duplicates SurrealDB connection management. Team of 1-3 cannot justify the operational overhead. No independent scaling need exists (single workspace, single developer workstation).
    20→
    21→### Alternative 2: LiteLLM as proxy layer with Brain plugin
    22→- **What**: Deploy LiteLLM (Python) as the forwarding proxy, write a Brain plugin for graph storage
    23→- **Expected impact**: Proven proxy with 100+ provider support
    24→- **Why insufficient**: Introduces Python dependency into a TypeScript/Bun stack. Plugin API does not support SurrealDB graph writes or Brain's policy evaluation pipeline. Cross-language integration complexity exceeds building the proxy in TypeScript. LiteLLM's cost tracking uses PostgreSQL, not a knowledge graph.
    25→
    26→### Alternative 3: Reverse proxy (nginx/Caddy) with webhook callbacks
    27→- **What**: nginx/Caddy handles SSE relay, sends webhook to Brain after each request
    28→- **Expected impact**: Zero custom code for SSE passthrough
    29→- **Why insufficient**: Cannot perform pre-request policy evaluation (budget, model access). Webhook callbacks cannot extract SSE usage data mid-stream. Adds nginx/Caddy as infrastructure dependency.
    30→
    31→## Consequences
    32→- **Positive**: Zero inter-process overhead; shared SurrealDB connection; reuses policy engine, observation system, inflight tracker; single deployment unit; simple local development
    33→- **Positive**: Proxy routes registered alongside API routes in `start-server.ts`; same `withRequestLogging` wrapper for observability
    34→- **Negative**: Proxy failure could affect main API server (mitigated by isolating proxy logic in pure functions with explicit error boundaries)
    35→- **Negative**: Cannot scale proxy independently of API server (acceptable for current team size and single-workspace deployment)
    36→

1→# Research: Brain LLM Proxy -- Transparent Proxy for Agent Trace Capture, Policy Enforcement, and Cost Attribution
     2→
     3→**Date**: 2026-03-15
     4→**Researcher**: nw-researcher (Nova)
     5→**Overall Confidence**: Medium-High
     6→**Sources Consulted**: 28
     7→
     8→## Executive Summary
     9→
    10→This research investigates the technical feasibility and implementation patterns for building a Brain LLM proxy -- a transparent intermediary between Claude Code (and other coding agents) and Anthropic's API. The proxy would capture full request/response traces as graph nodes, enforce policies before forwarding requests, attribute costs to workspaces/projects/tasks, and handle streaming SSE responses.
    11→
    12→The research establishes three key findings: (1) Claude Code natively supports proxy routing via `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` and related environment variables, making transparent interception straightforward with no client modifications; (2) the LLM proxy pattern is well-established in production, with LiteLLM, Helicone, and others proving the architecture at scale for cost tracking, policy enforcement, and observability; (3) SSE streaming passthrough is technically feasible using standard HTTP pipe/forward patterns, with the proxy acting as a transparent relay that can inspect events without buffering the full response.
    13→
    14→The proposed Brain LLM proxy would differentiate from generic LLM gateways by writing traces directly into the Brain knowledge graph as first-class entities -- linking LLM calls to tasks, decisions, and agent sessions rather than isolated log entries.
    15→
    16→---
    17→
    18→## Research Methodology
    19→
    20→**Search Strategy**: Web searches across official documentation (Anthropic, LiteLLM, OpenTelemetry), GitHub repositories (claude-code, litellm, llm-interceptor, llm-proxy), and industry sources. Local codebase analysis for Brain architecture context.
    21→
    22→**Source Selection Criteria**:
    23→- Source types: official documentation, open-source repositories, industry technical blogs
    24→- Reputation threshold: medium-high minimum
    25→- Verification method: cross-referencing across 3+ independent sources per major claim
    26→
    27→**Quality Standards**:
    28→- Minimum sources per claim: 3
    29→- Cross-reference requirement: all major claims
    30→- Source reputation: average score 0.78
    31→
    32→---
    33→
    34→## Findings
    35→
    36→### Finding 1: Claude Code Natively Supports Proxy Routing via Environment Variables
    37→
    38→**Evidence**: Claude Code provides first-class support for LLM gateways through environment variables. The primary configuration mechanism is `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL`, which redirects all API traffic to a custom endpoint.
    39→
    40→**Confidence**: High
    41→
    42→**Verification**: Cross-referenced with:
    43→- [Claude Code LLM Gateway Documentation](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/llm-gateway) -- official Anthropic docs
    44→- [Claude Code Enterprise Network Configuration](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/corporate-proxy) -- official Anthropic docs
    45→- [Claude Code Issue #216: Custom API Endpoint Support](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/216) -- GitHub
    46→- [Claude Code Issue #29015: Fast mode disabled with ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/29015) -- GitHub
    47→- [LiteLLM Claude Code Quickstart](https://docs.litellm.ai/docs/tutorials/claude_responses_api) -- LiteLLM docs
    48→
    49→**Key Environment Variables**:
    50→
    51→| Variable | Purpose |
    52→|----------|---------|
    53→| `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` | Redirect all API traffic to proxy URL (e.g., `http://localhost:4000`) |
    54→| `ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN` | Custom value for Authorization header (prefixed with `Bearer`) |
    55→| `ANTHROPIC_CUSTOM_HEADERS` | Additional headers in `Name: Value\nName2: Value2` format |
    56→| `ANTHROPIC_MODEL` | Override model name |
    57→| `ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_SONNET_MODEL` | Override Sonnet-class model ID |
    58→| `ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_HAIKU_MODEL` | Override Haiku-class model ID |
    59→| `ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_OPUS_MODEL` | Override Opus-class model ID |
    60→| `ANTHROPIC_SMALL_FAST_MODEL` | Override Haiku-class background task model |
    61→
    62→**Gateway Requirements** (from official Claude Code docs):
    63→- Must expose `/v1/messages` and `/v1/messages/count_tokens` endpoints
    64→- Must forward `anthropic-beta` and `anthropic-version` request headers
    65→- Must support SSE streaming responses
    66→- Configuration can be set via shell environment, `settings.json`, or per-project settings
    67→
    68→**Known Limitation**: When using `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL`, Claude Code's fast mode availability check is hardcoded to `https://api.anthropic.com` and does not respect the custom base URL, causing fast mode to be forcibly disabled [GitHub Issue #29015].
    69→
    70→**Analysis**: This is the most critical finding for feasibility. Claude Code already expects to talk to a proxy -- the Brain proxy simply needs to implement the Anthropic Messages API contract. No client-side patches or forks required.
    71→
    72→---
    73→
    74→### Finding 2: The Anthropic Messages API Has a Well-Defined Contract for Proxying
    75→
    76→**Evidence**: The Anthropic Messages API follows a predictable request/response contract that a proxy can intercept, inspect, and forward without transformation.
    77→
    78→**Confidence**: High
    79→
    80→**Verification**: Cross-referenced with:
    81→- [Anthropic Messages API Reference](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/api/messages) -- official
    82→- [Anthropic Streaming Messages Documentation](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/streaming) -- official
    83→- [Anthropic Count Tokens API](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/api/messages-count-tokens) -- official
    84→- [Anthropic API Overview](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/api/overview) -- official
    85→
    86→**Request Format** (POST `/v1/messages`):
    87→```json
    88→{
    89→  "model": "claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
    90→  "max_tokens": 1024,
    91→  "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "..."}],
    92→  "stream": true,
    93→  "system": "...",
    94→  "metadata": {"user_id": "..."}
    95→}
    96→```
    97→
    98→**Non-Streaming Response** includes:
    99→- `id`: message ID
   100→- `model`: model used
   101→- `usage`: `{ input_tokens, output_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens, cache_read_input_tokens }`
   102→- `stop_reason`: why generation stopped
   103→- `content`: array of content blocks
   104→
   105→**Streaming SSE Event Sequence**:
   106→1. `message_start` -- message ID, model, initial input token count
   107→2. `content_block_start` -- start of each content block with index
   108→3. `content_block_delta` -- incremental text/tool deltas (repeated)
   109→4. `content_block_stop` -- end of content block
   110→5. `message_delta` -- final usage stats (`output_tokens`, `stop_reason`)
   111→6. `message_stop` -- stream complete
   112→7. `ping` -- keepalive (can appear anywhere)
   113→
   114→**Response Headers**:
   115→- `request-id`: globally unique request identifier
   116→- `anthropic-organization-id`: org associated with API key
   117→- Rate limit headers: current usage, limits, reset times
   118→
   119→**Analysis**: The API contract is clean and well-documented. For a passthrough proxy, the key insight is that usage/cost data is available in both streaming (in `message_delta` event) and non-streaming (in `usage` field) responses. The proxy can capture this without buffering the full response.
   120→
   121→---
   122→
   123→### Finding 3: SSE Streaming Passthrough Is a Solved Problem
   124→
   125→**Evidence**: Multiple production systems demonstrate SSE passthrough for LLM APIs. The pattern involves the proxy establishing a connection to the upstream provider, then relaying SSE events to the client chunk-by-chunk while optionally inspecting each event.
   126→
   127→**Confidence**: High
   128→
   129→**Verification**: Cross-referenced with:
   130→- [LiteLLM Anthropic Passthrough](https://docs.litellm.ai/docs/pass_through/anthropic_completion) -- production implementation
   131→- [Claude Code Internals: SSE Stream Processing](https://kotrotsos.medium.com/claude-code-internals-part-7-sse-stream-processing-c620ae9d64a1) -- technical analysis
   132→- [anthropic-proxy-rs](https://github.com/m0n0x41d/anthropic-proxy-rs) -- Rust implementation with SSE passthrough
   133→- [llm-interceptor](https://github.com/chouzz/llm-interceptor) -- MITM proxy for Claude Code with SSE capture
   134→
   135→**Implementation Pattern** (pseudocode for Bun):
   136→```typescript
   137→// Proxy handler for POST /v1/messages
   138→async function proxyMessages(req: Request): Promise<Response> {
   139→  const body = await req.json();
   140→  const isStreaming = body.stream === true;
   141→
   142→  // Pre-request: policy check, metadata injection
   143→  await enforcePolicy(body);
   144→  const traceId = createTraceNode(body);
   145→
   146→  // Forward to Anthropic
   147→  const upstream = await fetch("https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages", {
   148→    method: "POST",
   149→    headers: forwardHeaders(req),
   150→    body: JSON.stringify(body),
   151→  });
   152→
   153→  if (!isStreaming) {
   154→    const response = await upstream.json();
   155→    await captureTrace(traceId, body, response);
   156→    return Response.json(response, { headers: upstream.headers });
   157→  }
   158→
   159→  // Streaming: pipe through with event inspection
   160→  const { readable, writable } = new TransformStream();
   161→  const writer = writable.getWriter();
   162→  const reader = upstream.body.getReader();
   163→  const decoder = new TextDecoder();
   164→
   165→  (async () => {
   166→    let buffer = "";
   167→    while (true) {
   168→      const { done, value } = await reader.read();
   169→      if (done) break;
   170→      const chunk = decoder.decode(value, { stream: true });
   171→      buffer += chunk;
   172→      // Inspect events for usage data (message_delta)
   173→      inspectSSEEvents(traceId, buffer);
   174→      writer.write(value); // Forward raw bytes
   175→    }
   176→    writer.close();
   177→    finalizeTrace(traceId);
   178→  })();
   179→
   180→  return new Response(readable, {
   181→    headers: {
   182→      "Content-Type": "text/event-stream",
   183→      "Cache-Control": "no-cache",
   184→      "Connection": "keep-alive",
   185→    },
   186→  });
   187→}
   188→```
   189→
   190→**Key Technical Considerations**:
   191→- SSE events can arrive split across chunks; buffer handling is required for partial events
   192→- Forward raw bytes to avoid encoding/decoding overhead on the hot path
   193→- Inspect events asynchronously -- extract `message_delta` for usage stats without blocking relay
   194→- Set `X-Accel-Buffering: no` to prevent nginx/reverse-proxy buffering
   195→
   196→**Analysis**: SSE passthrough adds minimal latency (microseconds for event inspection, no full-response buffering required). The proxy can extract cost data from the `message_delta` event at the end of the stream without impacting perceived latency.
   197→
   198→---
   199→
   200→### Finding 4: LiteLLM Proves the Cost Tracking and Attribution Architecture at Scale
   201→
   202→**Evidence**: LiteLLM is the most widely deployed open-source LLM proxy, handling cost tracking, budget enforcement, and attribution across 100+ model providers. Its architecture provides a proven reference for Brain's proxy design.
   203→
   204→**Confidence**: High
   205→
   206→**Verification**: Cross-referenced with:
   207→- [LiteLLM Cost Tracking Documentation](https://docs.litellm.ai/docs/proxy/cost_tracking) -- official
   208→- [LiteLLM Virtual Keys](https://docs.litellm.ai/docs/proxy/virtual_keys) -- official
   209→- [LiteLLM Budget Enforcement](https://docs.litellm.ai/docs/proxy/users) -- official
   210→- [LiteLLM Tag Budgets](https://docs.litellm.ai/docs/proxy/tag_budgets) -- official
   211→- [LiteLLM Architecture: Life of a Request](https://docs.litellm.ai/docs/proxy/architecture) -- official
   212→- [LiteLLM DB Schema](https://docs.litellm.ai/docs/proxy/db_info) -- official
   213→
   214→**LiteLLM Request Flow**:
   215→1. **Authentication**: Validate Bearer token (virtual key) against `LiteLLM_VerificationTokenTable`
   216→2. **Budget Check**: Verify key/user/team spend is under configured `max_budget`
   217→3. **Routing**: Select LLM deployment via Router (load balancing, fallbacks)
   218→4. **Forward**: Send request to provider in native format
   219→5. **Cost Calculation**: Compute cost from response `usage` (model-specific pricing)
   220→6. **Spend Logging**: Async write to `LiteLLM_SpendLogs` (non-blocking)
   221→7. **Budget Update**: Increment spend on key, user, team records
   222→
   223→**Attribution Hierarchy**:
   224→```
   225→Organization
   226→  -> Team (budget, rate limits)
   227→    -> Virtual Key (budget, rate limits, metadata)
   228→      -> User (budget tracking)
   229→        -> Tags (cost center, project, feature)
   230→```
   231→
   232→**Cost Calculation**: LiteLLM maintains a model-cost map with per-model input/output token prices. After each response, cost = `(input_tokens * input_price) + (output_tokens * output_price) + (cache_creation_tokens * cache_write_price) + (cache_read_tokens * cache_read_price)`.
   233→
   234→**Key Design Decision -- Async Spend Logging**: Spend logs are written asynchronously via `DBSpendUpdateWriter` with batched database writes. This prevents cost tracking from adding latency to the LLM response path.
   235→
   236→**Analysis**: Brain's proxy can adopt this pattern but map it to the knowledge graph instead of PostgreSQL. The attribution hierarchy maps cleanly: Organization -> Workspace, Team -> Project, Virtual Key -> Agent Session, Tags -> Task/Feature IDs. The async logging pattern is critical -- the proxy must not block SSE relay for graph writes.
   237→

   241→
   242→**Evidence**: Multiple LLM proxy implementations demonstrate pre-request policy enforcement, including content filtering, budget enforcement, rate limiting, and access control.
   243→
   244→**Confidence**: Medium-High
   245→
   246→**Verification**: Cross-referenced with:
   247→- [TrueFoundry: What Is LLM Proxy?](https://www.truefoundry.com/blog/llm-proxy) -- industry
   248→- [LiteLLM Guardrails](https://docs.litellm.ai/docs/) -- official

   251→
   252→**Policy Enforcement Points**:
   253→
   254→| Phase | Enforcement | Example |
   255→|-------|------------|---------|
   256→| Pre-request | Budget check | Reject if workspace spend exceeds limit |
   257→| Pre-request | Rate limiting | Enforce requests/minute per agent session |

   263→| Post-response | Anomaly detection | Flag unusual token consumption patterns |
   264→
   265→**Brain-Specific Policy Integration**: Brain already has a policy engine (`policy/` module) with typed rules, scopes, and lifecycle management. The proxy can evaluate intents against the policy graph before forwarding requests -- consistent with the existing "Judge" pattern where high-stakes actions go through an Authorizer Agent.
   266→
   267→**Analysis**: The proxy becomes a natural enforcement point for Brain's existing governance model. Rather than building a new policy system, the proxy queries the same policy graph that governs other agent actions.

   271→### Finding 6: Graph-Native Trace Storage Differentiates Brain from Generic Proxies
   272→
   273→**Evidence**: Current LLM observability tools (Langfuse, Datadog LLM Observability, OpenTelemetry GenAI conventions) store traces as flat logs or span trees. Brain's knowledge graph enables richer trace relationships.
   274→
   275→**Confidence**: Medium
   276→
   277→**Verification**: Cross-referenced with:
   278→- [Langfuse LLM Observability](https://langfuse.com/docs/observability/overview) -- OSS platform
   279→- [OpenTelemetry GenAI Semantic Conventions](https://opentelemetry.io/docs/specs/semconv/gen-ai/) -- standard
   280→- [Datadog LLM Observability](https://www.datadoghq.com/product/llm-observability/) -- commercial
   281→- [OpenTelemetry GenAI Spans](https://opentelemetry.io/docs/specs/semconv/gen-ai/gen-ai-spans/) -- standard
   282→
   283→**OpenTelemetry GenAI Attributes** (standardized):
   284→- `gen_ai.system`: provider name (e.g., "anthropic")
   285→- `gen_ai.request.model`: model ID
   286→- `gen_ai.usage.input_tokens`: input token count
   287→- `gen_ai.usage.output_tokens`: output token count
   288→- `gen_ai.response.finish_reasons`: stop reason
   289→
   290→**Brain Graph Trace Model** (proposed):
   291→
   292→Unlike flat span trees, Brain traces would be graph nodes with edges to related entities:
   293→
   294→```
   295→trace (SCHEMAFULL)
   296→  -> model: string
   297→  -> input_tokens: int
   298→  -> output_tokens: int
   299→  -> cache_creation_tokens: int
   300→  -> cache_read_tokens: int
   301→  -> cost_usd: float
   302→  -> latency_ms: int
   303→  -> stop_reason: string
   304→  -> request_id: string (Anthropic's request-id header)
   305→  -> created_at: datetime
   306→
   307→Relationships:
   308→  agent_session -> invoked -> trace
   309→  trace -> attributed_to -> task | feature | project
   310→  trace -> scoped_to -> workspace
   311→  trace -> governed_by -> policy
   312→```
   313→
   314→This enables queries like:
   315→- "Total cost of all LLM calls for task X"
   316→- "Which agent sessions consumed the most tokens this week?"
   317→- "Show all LLM calls that were governed by policy Y"
   318→- "Trace the full execution path from intent to LLM call to code change"
   319→
   320→**Analysis**: This is Brain's key differentiator. Generic proxies treat LLM calls as logs; Brain treats them as first-class knowledge graph entities with semantic relationships to the work they support. INTERPRETATION: This integration would close the observability gap where coding agent LLM usage is currently invisible to the Brain graph.

   350→### Finding 8: Anthropic Pricing Structure Enables Precise Cost Attribution
   351→
   352→**Evidence**: Anthropic's pricing is transparent and model-specific, with separate rates for input, output, cache write, and cache read tokens.
   353→
   354→**Confidence**: High
   355→
   356→**Verification**: Cross-referenced with:
   357→- [Anthropic Pricing](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/about-claude/pricing) -- official
   358→- [Anthropic Usage and Cost API](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/api/usage-cost-api) -- official
   359→- [PricePerToken Anthropic Pricing](https://pricepertoken.com/pricing-page/provider/anthropic) -- aggregator
   360→- [Amnic: Anthropic API Pricing Explained](https://amnic.com/blogs/anthropic-api-pricing) -- industry
   361→
   362→**Current Pricing** (per million tokens, as of 2026-03):
   363→
   364→| Model | Input | Output | Cache Write | Cache Read |
   365→|-------|-------|--------|-------------|------------|
   366→| Claude Haiku 3.5 | $0.80 | $4.00 | $1.00 | $0.08 |
   367→| Claude Sonnet 4 | $3.00 | $15.00 | $3.75 | $0.30 |
   368→| Claude Opus 4 | $15.00 | $75.00 | $18.75 | $1.50 |
   369→
   370→**Cost Calculation Formula**:
   371→```
   372→cost = (input_tokens * input_rate / 1_000_000)
   373→     + (output_tokens * output_rate / 1_000_000)
   374→     + (cache_creation_input_tokens * cache_write_rate / 1_000_000)
   375→     + (cache_read_input_tokens * cache_read_rate / 1_000_000)
   376→```
   377→
   378→**Where to Extract Usage Data**:
   379→- Non-streaming: `response.usage` object
   380→- Streaming: `message_delta` SSE event contains `usage` with `output_tokens`; `message_start` contains `usage` with `input_tokens`
   381→
   382→**Analysis**: The proxy can compute exact costs per request using the model name from the request body and token counts from the response. A local pricing table (updated periodically) avoids external API calls for cost calculation.

   386→## Proposed Architecture
   387→
   388→Based on the research findings, the Brain LLM proxy would follow this architecture:
   389→
   390→```
   391→Claude Code / MCP Agent
   392→  |
   393→  | ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL=http://localhost:{PORT}/anthropic
   394→  |
   395→  v
   396→Brain LLM Proxy (Bun server)
   397→  |
   398→  |-- [Pre-Request Pipeline]
   399→  |   |-- Authenticate (workspace token / agent session)
   400→  |   |-- Resolve attribution (workspace -> project -> task)
   401→  |   |-- Evaluate policies (query Brain policy graph)
   402→  |   |-- Rate limit check
   403→  |   |-- Budget check (workspace/project spend limits)
   404→  |   |-- Create trace node (graph: trace, status: pending)
   405→  |
   406→  |-- [Forward to Anthropic]
   407→  |   |-- Relay request with original headers (anthropic-beta, anthropic-version)
   408→  |   |-- Streaming: pipe SSE events through TransformStream
   409→  |   |-- Non-streaming: forward JSON response
   410→  |
   411→  |-- [Post-Response Pipeline] (async, non-blocking)
   412→  |   |-- Extract usage from response/stream events
   413→  |   |-- Calculate cost (local pricing table)
   414→  |   |-- Update trace node (tokens, cost, latency, stop_reason)
   415→  |   |-- Update spend counters (workspace, project, task)
   416→  |   |-- Emit observation if anomalous (high cost, unusual pattern)
   417→  |
   418→  v
   419→Anthropic API (api.anthropic.com)
   420→```
   421→
   422→**Key Design Decisions**:
   423→
   424→1. **Passthrough, not transformation**: Forward Anthropic API payloads as-is. No protocol translation. This ensures compatibility with all Claude Code features (extended thinking, tool use, computer use).
   425→
   426→2. **Async post-processing**: Graph writes and cost updates happen asynchronously after the response is relayed. The proxy adds zero perceptible latency to the LLM call.
   427→
   428→3. **Attribution via headers**: Claude Code's `ANTHROPIC_CUSTOM_HEADERS` can carry `X-Brain-Workspace`, `X-Brain-Task`, `X-Brain-Session` headers for attribution without modifying the Anthropic API payload.
   429→
   430→4. **SurrealDB graph storage**: Traces stored as `trace` nodes with `RELATE` edges to agent sessions, tasks, and workspaces. Consistent with Brain's existing graph model.
   431→
   432→5. **Policy graph integration**: Pre-request policy checks query the same policy engine used by Brain's Authorizer. No separate policy system.
   433→

The issue likely contains the original feature request and discussion context that motivated this research. Its contents should be appended to this document once retrieved.
   486→
   487→### Gap 2: Brain's Existing Agent Session Tracing Model
   488→
   489→**Issue**: The research does not fully map how Brain's existing `agent_session` and hierarchical trace model (described in README) would integrate with LLM-level traces. The exact schema for agent sessions and trace nodes was not examined in detail.

   495→### Gap 3: Extended Thinking and Tool Use Token Accounting
   496→
   497→**Issue**: Claude's extended thinking feature and multi-turn tool use create complex token accounting scenarios (thinking tokens, tool call tokens, tool result tokens). It is unclear how these map to the standard `input_tokens`/`output_tokens` fields in streaming responses.

   499→**Attempted Sources**: Anthropic streaming docs (partial coverage), Claude Code internals analysis.

   501→**Recommendation**: Test with actual Claude API calls using extended thinking enabled and capture the full SSE event stream to document exact token accounting behavior.
   502→
   503→### Gap 4: Concurrent Agent Session Attribution
   504→
   505→**Issue**: When multiple Claude Code instances run concurrently (e.g., multiple terminal sessions), the proxy needs to attribute requests to the correct agent session. The exact mechanism for injecting session-specific attribution headers is not fully specified.

   507→**Attempted Sources**: Claude Code environment variable documentation, `ANTHROPIC_CUSTOM_HEADERS` behavior.
   508→
   509→**Recommendation**: Test whether `ANTHROPIC_CUSTOM_HEADERS` can be set per-process (e.g., via `brain start task:X` injecting session-specific headers), or whether an alternative attribution mechanism (e.g., per-session API keys / virtual keys) is needed.

   513→## Conflicting Information
   514→
   515→### Conflict 1: Fast Mode Behavior with Custom Base URL
   516→
   517→**Position A**: Claude Code gateway documentation states that LLM gateways are fully supported with `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL`.
   518→- Source: [Claude Code LLM Gateway Docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/llm-gateway) - Reputation: High
   519→
   520→**Position B**: Fast mode is forcibly disabled when using `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` because the availability check is hardcoded to `api.anthropic.com`.
   521→- Source: [GitHub Issue #29015](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/29015) - Reputation: High
   522→
   523→**Assessment**: Both are correct -- gateways are supported, but with reduced functionality. This is a known limitation. For Brain's proxy, this means agents using the proxy will not have access to fast mode (which uses batched/cheaper inference). This may be acceptable if the proxy provides compensating value (tracing, policy enforcement, cost tracking). The issue may be resolved in future Claude Code releases.
